title: Indonesia Prices $1 Billion Panda Bond as Yuan Debt Surges, Houthis Strike Saudi Tankers, ECB Holds
subhead: Indonesia launches its first panda bond as yuan-denominated debt issuance surges 60% in 2026, deepening China's financial hold on emerging markets. Houthi rebels attacked two Saudi oil tankers in the Red Sea — the first kinetic strikes since their blockade declaration — pushing Brent above $95. The ECB held rates at 2.25% but kept the door wide open for a September hike.

sections.heading: Indonesia Prices $1 Billion Panda Bond as Yuan Debt Market Surges
sections.body:
Indonesia launched its inaugural panda bond on Thursday, raising $1 billion in yuan-denominated debt in China's onshore market. Finance Minister Purbaya Yudhi Sadewa announced the offering on Tuesday, framing it as a diversification of funding sources amid rupiah volatility and fiscal pressure from the Iran war's commodity shock.

Indonesia is the latest sovereign to tap what has become a booming market. Panda bond issuance rose 60% in the first half of 2026, exceeding 160 billion yuan ($23.6 billion), according to People's Bank of China statistics. The attraction is clear: China's accommodative monetary policy lets overseas issuers borrow at rates below 3% — significantly cheaper than dollar or euro markets.

Kazakhstan became the first Central Asian country to issue panda bonds earlier this month, raising 3 billion yuan ($443 million) through its sovereign wealth fund Samruk-Kazyna, which manages $88.6 billion in assets. Pakistan has also raised the equivalent of $250 million through its first panda bond. Even Russia launched its second round of yuan-denominated sovereign bonds in June, weeks after Putin met Xi in Beijing.

China's regulators moved on Tuesday to mandate credit rating reform by August 1, requiring rating agencies to comply with new standards for panda bond issuers — a signal that Beijing wants to professionalise the market as it scales up. Natixis chief economist Alicia Garcia-Herrero told RFE/RL that deeper panda bond integration 'ties repayment and refinancing to yuan dynamics and Chinese policy, giving Beijing leverage through potential refinancing terms, trade linkages... and diplomatic alignment.'
sections.whyItMatters: Panda bonds are no longer a niche instrument. With 60% issuance growth, credit rating reform, and sovereign issuers from Kazakhstan to Indonesia lining up, the yuan debt market is becoming a genuine alternative to dollar-denominated financing — and Beijing is acquiring diplomatic leverage over every issuer in the process.

sections.heading: Houthis Strike Two Saudi Tankers in Red Sea, Escalating Beyond Threats
sections.body:
Yemen's Houthi rebels claimed to have struck two Saudi oil tankers — the Encelia and the Layla — with missiles and drones in the Red Sea on Wednesday, marking the first kinetic attacks on commercial vessels since the group declared a naval blockade against Saudi Arabia earlier this week. The UK Maritime Trade Operations centre reported a tanker was hit by an unknown projectile approximately 70 nautical miles southwest of Al Shuqaiq, sparking a fire that the crew was fighting.

The attack represents a qualitative escalation: the Houthis have moved from broadcasting radio warnings and declaring a blockade to actually striking vessels. According to maritime data analysed by AFP, at least nine ships turned back from crossing the Bab el-Mandeb Strait after the blockade announcement, including three that had loaded oil at Saudi Arabia's Red Sea terminal at Yanbu. However, nearly 30 ships still crossed the strait on Tuesday, according to shipping tracker Kpler.

President Trump responded with a direct threat: 'Any time the Islamic Republic of Iran shoots at a ship in the Strait of Hormuz, the United States will bomb and destroy ONE BRIDGE OR POWER PLANT.' Iran's Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi warned of an 'eye for an eye' response, saying Tehran would strike US-linked infrastructure and energy facilities across the region if Washington carried out the attacks. Iran's state broadcaster said US threats 'will have no result other than the expansion of the war.'

US Central Command reported the 12th consecutive night of strikes on Iran, while Kuwait's military said its air defences were intercepting hostile drones, Jordan shot down four missiles and four drones, and air raid sirens sounded in Bahrain.
sections.whyItMatters: The Houthi attack on actual tankers — not just threats — means both major Middle Eastern shipping chokepoints are now under active fire for the first time in modern maritime history. Trump's 'bridge for a bridge' escalation doctrine and Iran's 'eye for an eye' response raise the risk of a further spiral that could rapidly pull Gulf states deeper into the conflict.

sections.heading: Brent Tops $95 as Dual Chokepoint Risk Intensifies
sections.body:
Brent crude climbed as much as 1.4% to trade above $95 a barrel on Thursday, the highest level in nearly six weeks, while WTI advanced around 1.7% to $88.27. The moves followed the Houthi tanker strikes and Trump's escalation threat against Iranian infrastructure.

HSBC senior oil and gas analyst Kim Fustier said the recent rally reflects renewed concerns over the Strait of Hormuz after the breakdown of the US-Iran ceasefire. 'Since July 7-8, the ceasefire has frayed as Iranian attacks on vessels transiting the strait prompted US retaliatory strikes,' Fustier said. 'The core issue remains unresolved: whether passage is administered, and by whom. In hindsight, the more traffic was rising through the US-managed Omani lane, the less this outcome suited Iran.'

The price surge comes as gold climbed to $4,125 per ounce — a 3.6% daily gain on Tuesday — with investors seeking safe-haven assets amid the Middle East escalation and ahead of next week's Fed meeting. The 10-year Treasury yield hit a two-month high, tracking its fourth consecutive session of gains as oil-driven inflation expectations reinforced the Fed's hawkish stance. The dollar index edged up 0.2% to 101.17.
sections.whyItMatters: Brent above $95 with both Hormuz and Bab el-Mandeb under active military threat means the oil market is now pricing in a scenario with no historical precedent — simultaneous disruption of the two most critical energy shipping chokepoints. The inflationary transmission into Treasury yields and gold signals that markets are beginning to price a protracted disruption, not a temporary spike.

sections.heading: ECB Holds at 2.25%, Leaves September Door Wide Open
sections.body:
The European Central Bank is all but certain to keep interest rates at 2.25% at its Thursday meeting, with interest rate swaps pricing an 87% probability of no change. Reuters reported that the ECB will 'hold the door wide open to another rate hike in September,' citing officials familiar with the deliberations.

The hold comes despite eurozone annual inflation falling to 2.8% in June from 3.2% in May — a decline that might suggest easing pressure. But the ECB's Governing Council is focused on the energy-driven inflation resurgence from the Middle East conflict, with markets assigning a 75% probability of a September hike and a 34% chance of another in October.

ING framed the decision as a 'hawkish-leaning hold,' noting that the September hike is already nearly fully priced into markets. The real question for Thursday's press conference is whether President Christine Lagarde explicitly signals September as the next move or maintains maximum flexibility.

The ECB is also considering doubling its minimum reserve requirement, which would withdraw approximately €160-170 billion of excess liquidity from the banking system — a form of stealth tightening that would amplify any future rate hike. The euro sat at $1.1403, down 0.1% against the dollar.
sections.whyItMatters: The ECB's hold is the least interesting part of today's decision. What matters is the forward guidance: with September 75% priced for a hike, reserve-requirement doubling on the table, and oil above $95, Lagarde's press conference will determine whether the market's aggressive pricing gets validated or pushed back.

sections.heading: Graham Act Nears Senate Floor Vote as Pence Calls for Passage
sections.body:
Former Vice President Mike Pence called on the Senate to pass the Lindsey O. Graham Sanctioning Russia Act of 2026 in 'overwhelming numbers,' joining a growing bipartisan push to honour the late senator by advancing the bill he helped negotiate. The legislation now has more than 26 co-sponsors and supporters expect that number to grow.

Senate Democratic Leader Chuck Schumer called for an immediate floor vote, saying the legislation should be passed 'in honour of Lindsey.' Senate Majority Leader John Thune said he was 'hopeful we can make that happen.' Senator Richard Blumenthal, Graham's chief Democratic partner on the bill, said he supports naming the legislation after Graham and expressed confidence: 'I think we have the votes.'

However, the bill faces opposition in the House. Representative Gregory Meeks, the top House Democrat on foreign policy, argued the legislation grants Trump excessive tariff authority rather than mandating automatic sanctions. 'This is not so much a sanctions bill as it is a massive backdoor authority for President Trump to impose more tariffs, including on our European allies,' Meeks said.

Meanwhile, OFAC updated two SDN entries for Russia-linked entities in the UAE and Saint Petersburg on July 20, and both the UK and EU announced new sanctions targeting Russia's shadow fleet and war supply chains, according to MSN. The Graham Act — with its tariffs of up to 100% on the top five purchasers of Russian energy — would go significantly further than existing measures.
sections.whyItMatters: The Graham Act is approaching a Senate floor vote with genuine bipartisan momentum, but the House is a different story. Meeks' critique highlights the central tension: the bill's secondary tariffs on Russian energy buyers like China and India could accelerate dedollarization even as they squeeze Moscow, and they also give Trump a broad new tariff instrument.

sections.heading: Canada Opens 30-Day Window as Section 338 Tariffs Loom
sections.body:
Canada's 30-day countdown before Trump's 50% tariffs under Section 338 take effect has begun, with the first week marked by competing signals. Prime Minister Mark Carney said he and Trump agreed to 'intensify trade negotiations,' but Trump separately threatened additional tariffs over wildfire smoke drifting into the US, calling it 'willful' pollution.

Ontario Premier Doug Ford called for 'tariff for tariff, dollar for dollar' retaliation, while the Canadian Chamber of Commerce urged both sides to use the 30-day window for meaningful negotiations. The Canadian dollar weakened 0.15% to C$1.409 per USD following the tariff announcement.

The tariffs — imposed under Section 338 of the Tariff Act of 1930, a provision never before used to impose duties — cover products previously protected under USMCA, including wine, cement, and manufactured goods, while exempting energy, potash, critical minerals, and fish. The US has also declined to extend USMCA in its current form, and US and Mexican negotiators launched a third round of bilateral USMCA talks in Mexico City on Tuesday.

Meanwhile, Carney raised the issue of China EV tariffs with Trump at the G7 summit, according to headline tracking — suggesting Canada is trying to find common ground with Washington on China policy even as the bilateral trade relationship fractures.
sections.whyItMatters: The 30-day clock is ticking. If no deal is reached by mid-August, 50% tariffs on America's second-largest trading partner take effect under an untested 1930s statute — with USMCA already expired and no replacement in place. The combination creates maximum leverage for the White House and maximum uncertainty for North American supply chains.

bottomLine: The Houthis moved from threatening a blockade to actually striking Saudi tankers in the Red Sea — the first kinetic escalation since the Bab el-Mandeb declaration. With both Hormuz and Bab el-Mandeb under fire and Brent above $95, the oil market is now pricing in a scenario with no modern precedent. Indonesia's $1 billion panda bond debut illustrates a quieter but consequential shift: yuan-denominated debt issuance is up 60% this year, and Beijing is systematically building financial infrastructure — credit rating reforms, sovereign issuers across Asia — that gives it leverage over every borrower. The ECB's hold today is a foregone conclusion; the real signal comes from Lagarde's guidance on September and the stealth tightening via reserve requirements. Meanwhile, the Graham Act nears a Senate vote with Pence's backing but faces House resistance over tariff authority, and Canada's 30-day tariff clock is running.
sources: CNBC, Reuters, RFE/RL, AFP, Malay Mail, Global Times, CryptoBriefing, Forbes, USA Today, MSN, HSBC, LBC, Newsday, Kpler, UKMTO, US Central Command, Bloomberg
